g.data-narration: Welcome to the F-Distribution module. We'll explore this key statistical distribution used for comparing variances.
The F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution used to compare two population variances. It's essential for ANOVA and regression analysis.
g.text: A continuous probability distribution that arises when comparing
two population variances. Key applications include:

g.data-narration: If X1 and X2 are independent chi-square variables with d1 and d2 degrees of freedom, then F equals the ratio of their scaled values.
g.text: If X₁ and X₂ are independent chi-square random variables:
d₁ = numerator df (v1), d₂ = denominator df (v2)

g.data-narration: The probability density function of F-distribution uses the Beta function. It describes how probability is distributed across positive values.
g.text: The distribution is right-skewed and defined only for x ≥ 0

g.data-narration: The F-distribution has key properties: it's non-negative, right-skewed, and its shape changes based on both degrees of freedom.
The mean of F-distribution equals d₂ divided by d₂ minus 2, existing only when d₂ exceeds 2. Variance has a more complex formula.
An important property: if F follows F(d1, d2), then its reciprocal 1/F follows F(d2, d1) with swapped degrees of freedom.
g.text: If F ~ F(d₁, d₂), then its reciprocal also follows an F-distribution:

g.data-narration: The F-distribution connects to other distributions: chi-square through its definition, t-distribution through squaring, and normal for large degrees.
g.text: F → N(1, σ) for large df

g.data-narration: The F-table provides critical values. Choose significance level α, find d1 as column header and d2 as row, then read the intersection value.
g.text: Decision: Reject H₀ if F > F_α(d₁,d₂)

g.data-narration: The F-test compares two population variances. For two-tailed tests, null hypothesis states equal variances; alternative states they're unequal.
g.text: F-Test for Equality of Two Variances
Convention: Place larger sample variance in numerator → F ≥ 1

g.data-narration: The F test statistic equals the ratio of the two sample variances. Degrees of freedom are n1-1 for numerator and n2-1 for denominator.
g.text: The test follows F(d₁, d₂) distribution under the null hypothesis

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example: Testing if two machines have equal variance using sample sizes 10 and 8, variances 0.045 and 0.018.
g.text: Two machines produce bolts. Sample of 10 from Machine A: s₁² = 0.045
Sample of 8 from Machine B: s₂² = 0.018. Test at α = 0.05 (two-tailed)
Conclusion: Since 2.5 < 4.82, FAIL TO REJECT H₀ - equal variances

g.data-narration: Example 2: Testing if Method B has lower variability than Method A. Using sample variances 120 and 50, we compute F equals 2.4.
g.text: Method A (n=16): s₁² = 120 | Method B (n=21): s₂² = 50. Test claim that B has lower
Conclusion: Since 2.4 > 2.20, REJECT H₀ - Method B has lower variability

g.data-narration: This summary table captures all key formulas and properties. Keep it handy for quick reference during exams and practical applications.
